source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 12.4 The crontab command (pp. 213-214)

questions.q: Where is a user's schedule file stored when they create scheduled tasks?
questions.why: Each user's tasks live in /var/spool/cron/<user>; crond reads these files (the minimum recognised unit is 1 minute).
crontab -e opens your schedule file in vi for editing.

questions.q: Display the contents of your crontab file.
questions.why: crontab -l lists your scheduled entries without editing them.
crontab -r removes the schedule file entirely.

questions.q: As root, edit the crontab of the user user1.
questions.why: -u names another user's schedule file; only root may use -u.

questions.q: Why is running 'crontab' with NO options dangerous?
options: It deletes the old schedule file and waits for new input (you must press Ctrl+D to exit)
questions.why: Bare crontab discards the existing file and reads new lines from stdin until Ctrl+D - easy to wipe your schedule by mistake (use -e).

questions.q: Which is a requirement for a program scheduled via crontab?
options: It must be autonomous and provide its own redirections (stdin/stdout/stderr) - not rely on terminal I/O
It must print to the current terminal
It must wait for keyboard input
It must be restarted after each run
questions.why: Scheduled jobs run unattended, so the program must be autonomous with redirected I/O; interactive terminal commands are unsuitable.
